base.name: AI Leapfrog
base.description: A scenario where NDC-driven retailing, Generative-AI travel agents, and airline direct-booking pressure compound over 2025–2040. Early movers that build or partner into AI concierges capture the consumer surface; laggards on traditional search-and-book UX commoditize. Airline direct booking crosses 50% by 2032, forcing OTAs to pivot to complex itineraries, become AI-first aggregators, or consolidate out.

timeline.label: Airbnb $1B valuation — home-sharing enters OTA airspace
Meta-search era; mobile majority of bookings
Google Flights / Hotel Ads push intensifies
Recovery to pre-COVID volume for leaders
GenAI emergence; Expedia Trip Planner / TripGenie launch
NDC 21.3 mainstream; AI agent pilots; OpenAI Operator GA

nodes.title: NDC + ONE Order Adoption
nodes.description: IATA NDC 21.3 and ONE Order go mainstream on major carriers (Lufthansa Group, British Airways, American, Singapore). Retailing shifts from GDS passive content to airline-controlled offer management. OTAs decide whether to build direct NDC pipelines — unlocking ancillary revenue (seats, bags, meals) at the cost of higher integration effort — or stay passive behind GDS aggregators and risk eroding content parity against airline.com.

branches.label: Embrace NDC + ONE Order
branches.description: Build direct NDC pipelines to the major carriers; integrate airline Offer & Order management; capture rich content and ancillary margin. Higher cost-to-serve per booking, but a defensible content-parity moat.

branches.label: Stay GDS-first
branches.description: Maintain Amadeus / Sabre / Travelport as the primary channel; mitigate content gaps with aggregator partnerships. Lower upfront cost; gradual content parity erosion vs direct airline.com over 2–3 years.

nodes.title: AI Agent Gateway Decision
nodes.description: Generative-AI-powered travel agents (OpenAI Operator, Anthropic computer-use, Perplexity Travel, Google Gemini Travel) move from pilot to consumer default. Whoever sits between the user and the inventory captures the consumer relationship — and the margin. OTAs decide whether to build the agent themselves, partner with a platform provider, or defer the bet and protect the legacy search-and-book funnel.

branches.label: Build Proprietary AI Concierge
branches.description: Heavy R&D into in-house conversational booking (successor generations of Expedia Trip Planner, Trip.com TripGenie, Priceline Penny). Owns the consumer surface; defensible moat if adoption lands.

branches.label: Partner with AI Gatekeepers
branches.description: Integrate as the travel-fulfillment backend for OpenAI / Anthropic / Google agents. Fast to market, low R&D burn; gives up the consumer-facing surface to whoever owns the assistant.

branches.label: Defer AI Investment
branches.description: Double down on search-and-book UX and performance marketing. Lowest short-term cost; highest risk of being routed around once agents reach mainstream adoption (2029–2031 central estimate).

nodes.title: Airline Direct-Booking Tipping Point
nodes.description: Airlines capture >50% of flight bookings directly on domestic routes — propelled by NDC, loyalty-program lock-in, and AI agents that now query airline.com directly. OTAs no longer 'own' the flight transaction and must redefine the value proposition or consolidate.

branches.label: Pivot to Complex Itineraries
branches.description: Focus on multi-city, multi-modal (flight + rail + car + lodge), multi-PNR itineraries that airlines and their AI agents cannot serve directly. Higher margin per trip; smaller addressable market.

branches.label: Become AI-First Aggregator
branches.description: Transform into a marketplace API layer that suppliers plug into and consumer-facing AI agents query. Volume play in a post-consumer-UX world; margin compresses but scale is enormous.

branches.label: Consolidate or Exit
branches.description: M&A to gain scale, sell to a hyperscaler, or exit the market as unit economics compress beyond viable return on ad spend.

strategic_profiles.name: Cost Leader
strategic_profiles.description: Volume-and-scale play: wins on commoditized hotel and flight inventory via performance marketing, aggressive cancellation flexibility, and operational efficiency. Typical of budget- focused brands (Agoda, eDreams), subscription-led price players, and flight-only aggregators.

strategic_profiles.name: Differentiator
strategic_profiles.description: Content and experience play: invests in AI-driven personalization, premium UX (Trip Planner, AI concierges), unique inventory (Vrbo / homes), and brand. Defensible against airline direct-booking pressure because the value-add isn't just the ticket — it's itinerary, stay, and ancillary.

strategic_profiles.name: Niche Search
strategic_profiles.description: Technical-moat play: solves a hard specialist problem airlines and hyperscalers can't easily copy — multi-city virtual interlining (Kiwi.com), complex multi-PNR itineraries, regional dominance. Volume is small but margin per booking and customer loyalty are high.

nodes.title: EU AI Travel Regulation Compliance Crossroads
nodes.description: Following the EU's 2027 AI Act extension covering travel-agent systems (mandatory transparency on AI-generated recommendations, algorithmic auditing, supplier-neutrality disclosures, and consumer right-to-human-fallback), enforcement matures by 2035 with multi-million-euro fines being levied on non-compliant agent operators. OTAs operating in the EU — directly or via partner AI gatekeepers — must decide how deeply to re-architect their AI stacks, accept geographic fragmentation, or push back politically. The decision shapes consumer trust, R&D burn, and access to ~30% of the global travel-spend pool.

branches.label: Full Compliance & Trust Moat
branches.description: Re-architect AI concierge and aggregator APIs to meet EU AI Act requirements globally (transparency layers, audit logs, supplier-neutrality scoring, human-fallback). Costly but converts regulation into a trust-and-brand moat against under-regulated agent rivals; opens enterprise / corporate-travel upside.

branches.label: Two-Tier Geographic Stack
branches.description: Maintain a compliant, feature-restricted EU variant alongside a fuller global stack. Limits compliance cost but fragments product velocity, weakens EU consumer experience, and cedes EU share to compliance-native rivals (Trip.com EU, regional challengers).

branches.label: Lobby & Delay
branches.description: Join industry coalitions to challenge enforcement scope, accept selective fines as cost of doing business, defer deep re-architecture. Preserves short-term margin; high tail risk of class-action exposure and forced remediation if enforcement hardens.

nodes.title: EU AI Policy Mandate Compliance Crossroads
nodes.description: Following the EU's 2027 mandate requiring all consumer-facing travel platforms to publish, audit, and enforce formal AI policies (covering agent transparency, recommendation explainability, training-data provenance, and supplier-bias disclosure), the regulation has now matured through its 2030–2034 enforcement phase. By 2035, OTAs face a strategic choice on how to live with — or lean into — the regime as similar frameworks emerge in the UK, Brazil, and APAC. The decision shapes cost-to-serve, geographic footprint, and whether AI compliance becomes a moat or a tax.

branches.label: Lead on Compliance — Trust as Moat
branches.description: Invest aggressively in explainable-AI tooling, third-party audit trails, and 'EU-grade' AI policy as a global default. Higher opex, but trust-led brand differentiation that travels well into other regulated markets and into B2B/corporate-travel deals.

branches.label: Geo-Fence the EU Stack
branches.description: Maintain two AI stacks — a stripped-down compliant model for EU users and a faster-moving model elsewhere. Preserves global innovation velocity but fragments the product, raises engineering complexity, and concedes EU consumer experience to rivals who unified.

branches.label: Lobby for Harmonization + Minimal Compliance
branches.description: Meet the letter of the EU mandate with industry-standard compliance kits while pushing via trade bodies (ETOA, WTTC) for a harmonized global AI-travel framework. Lowest near-term cost; exposed to enforcement actions and reputational risk if an OTA-driven AI agent misfires publicly.

nodes.title: NDC + ONE Order Adoption
nodes.description: IATA NDC 21.3 and ONE Order go mainstream on major carriers (Lufthansa Group, British Airways, American, Singapore). Retailing shifts from GDS passive content to airline-controlled offer management. OTAs decide whether to build direct NDC pipelines — unlocking ancillary revenue (seats, bags, meals) at the cost of higher integration effort — or stay passive behind GDS aggregators and risk eroding content parity against airline.com.

branches.label: Embrace NDC + ONE Order
branches.description: Build direct NDC pipelines to the major carriers; integrate airline Offer & Order management; capture rich content and ancillary margin. Higher cost-to-serve per booking, but a defensible content-parity moat.

branches.label: Stay GDS-first
branches.description: Maintain Amadeus / Sabre / Travelport as the primary channel; mitigate content gaps with aggregator partnerships. Lower upfront cost; gradual content parity erosion vs direct airline.com over 2–3 years.

nodes.title: AI Agent Gateway Decision
nodes.description: Generative-AI-powered travel agents (OpenAI Operator, Anthropic computer-use, Perplexity Travel, Google Gemini Travel) move from pilot to consumer default. Whoever sits between the user and the inventory captures the consumer relationship — and the margin. OTAs decide whether to build the agent themselves, partner with a platform provider, or defer the bet and protect the legacy search-and-book funnel.

branches.label: Build Proprietary AI Concierge
branches.description: Heavy R&D into in-house conversational booking (successor generations of Expedia Trip Planner, Trip.com TripGenie, Priceline Penny). Owns the consumer surface; defensible moat if adoption lands.

branches.label: Partner with AI Gatekeepers
branches.description: Integrate as the travel-fulfillment backend for OpenAI / Anthropic / Google agents. Fast to market, low R&D burn; gives up the consumer-facing surface to whoever owns the assistant.

branches.label: Defer AI Investment
branches.description: Double down on search-and-book UX and performance marketing. Lowest short-term cost; highest risk of being routed around once agents reach mainstream adoption (2029–2031 central estimate).

branches.label: EU AI Act lands mid-rollout
branches.description: Brussels passes binding AI-agent regulation in 2028, forcing OTAs to layer a compliance decision on top of whichever AI strategy they chose.

branches.label: US AI regulation lands in 2030
branches.description: Washington passes federal AI-agent regulation in 2030, layering FTC-led disclosure and liability rules on top of whichever AI strategy the OTA chose at tp-002.

nodes.title: Airline Direct-Booking Tipping Point
nodes.description: Airlines capture >50% of flight bookings directly on domestic routes — propelled by NDC, loyalty-program lock-in, and AI agents that now query airline.com directly. OTAs no longer 'own' the flight transaction and must redefine the value proposition or consolidate.

branches.label: Pivot to Complex Itineraries
branches.description: Focus on multi-city, multi-modal (flight + rail + car + lodge), multi-PNR itineraries that airlines and their AI agents cannot serve directly. Higher margin per trip; smaller addressable market.

branches.label: Become AI-First Aggregator
branches.description: Transform into a marketplace API layer that suppliers plug into and consumer-facing AI agents query. Volume play in a post-consumer-UX world; margin compresses but scale is enormous.

branches.label: Consolidate or Exit
branches.description: M&A to gain scale, sell to a hyperscaler, or exit the market as unit economics compress beyond viable return on ad spend.

nodes.title: EU AI Act Enforcement on Travel Agents
nodes.description: The EU implements binding AI regulation specifically targeting consumer-facing AI agents: transparency mandates on agent-driven recommendations, liability for mis-sold itineraries, GDPR-plus data-handling rules, and audit requirements for ranking algorithms. OTAs operating in EU markets must decide whether to invest in compliance infrastructure, geofence AI features outside the EU, or pull AI concierges from the EU entirely and revert to classic search-and-book in-region.

branches.label: Full Compliance Investment
branches.description: Build EU-grade transparency, audit logs, and explainability into the AI agent stack; treat compliance as a moat against smaller competitors who cannot afford it. High upfront cost, slower feature velocity, but defensible position in a $200B+ EU travel market.

branches.label: Geofence AI Features
branches.description: Run the full AI concierge in non-EU markets and serve EU users a stripped-down, rules-based booking flow. Avoids compliance cost but cedes EU consumer surface to compliant competitors and to airline.com direct.

branches.label: Lobby + Delay
branches.description: Join industry coalitions to push for softer enforcement, ship minimum-viable compliance, and absorb early fines as cost of doing business. Buys 12-18 months but risks reputational damage and a harder regulatory posture in round two.

nodes.source_question: What if the EU implements an AI regulation in 2028?

nodes.title: US AI Regulation on OTAs
nodes.description: The US enacts federal AI regulation targeting consumer-facing travel agents: FTC-led transparency rules on AI-driven recommendations, disclosure requirements for algorithmic ranking and sponsored placement, liability for mis-sold itineraries, and state-level patchwork (CA, NY, IL) layering biometric and data-handling rules on top. Coming two years after the EU AI Act, US enforcement reshapes the home market for Booking Holdings, Expedia, and Airbnb. OTAs must decide whether to invest in compliance infrastructure, scale back AI agent autonomy in the US, or fight enforcement through litigation and lobbying.

branches.label: Full US Compliance Investment
branches.description: Build FTC-grade disclosure, audit trails, and explainability into the AI concierge stack; align with the strictest state regime (likely California) as a national baseline. High upfront cost but converts compliance into a moat against smaller AI-native challengers.

branches.label: Scale Back AI Autonomy in US
branches.description: Downgrade the US-facing AI agent to a recommendation-only assistant with human-confirmed bookings; preserve the autonomous concierge in non-US markets. Avoids the heaviest liability exposure but cedes the US consumer surface to airline.com direct and to compliant competitors.

branches.label: Litigate + Lobby
branches.description: Challenge enforcement in court, fund industry coalitions, and ship minimum-viable compliance while absorbing early fines. Buys 12-24 months of feature velocity but risks reputational damage and a harsher round-two regulatory posture.

user.name: AI Leapfrog (EU)
user.description: A scenario where NDC-driven retailing, Generative-AI travel agents, and airline direct-booking pressure compound over 2025–2040. Early movers that build or partner into AI concierges capture the consumer surface; laggards on traditional search-and-book UX commoditize. Airline direct booking crosses 50% by 2032, forcing OTAs to pivot to complex itineraries, become AI-first aggregators, or consolidate out.

nodes.title: NDC + ONE Order Adoption
nodes.description: IATA NDC 21.3 and ONE Order go mainstream on major carriers (Lufthansa Group, British Airways, American, Singapore). Retailing shifts from GDS passive content to airline-controlled offer management. OTAs decide whether to build direct NDC pipelines — unlocking ancillary revenue (seats, bags, meals) at the cost of higher integration effort — or stay passive behind GDS aggregators and risk eroding content parity against airline.com.

branches.label: Embrace NDC + ONE Order
branches.description: Build direct NDC pipelines to the major carriers; integrate airline Offer & Order management; capture rich content and ancillary margin. Higher cost-to-serve per booking, but a defensible content-parity moat.

branches.label: Stay GDS-first
branches.description: Maintain Amadeus / Sabre / Travelport as the primary channel; mitigate content gaps with aggregator partnerships. Lower upfront cost; gradual content parity erosion vs direct airline.com over 2–3 years.

nodes.title: AI Agent Gateway Decision
nodes.description: Generative-AI-powered travel agents (OpenAI Operator, Anthropic computer-use, Perplexity Travel, Google Gemini Travel) move from pilot to consumer default. Whoever sits between the user and the inventory captures the consumer relationship — and the margin. OTAs decide whether to build the agent themselves, partner with a platform provider, or defer the bet and protect the legacy search-and-book funnel.

branches.label: Build Proprietary AI Concierge
branches.description: Heavy R&D into in-house conversational booking (successor generations of Expedia Trip Planner, Trip.com TripGenie, Priceline Penny). Owns the consumer surface; defensible moat if adoption lands.

branches.label: Partner with AI Gatekeepers
branches.description: Integrate as the travel-fulfillment backend for OpenAI / Anthropic / Google agents. Fast to market, low R&D burn; gives up the consumer-facing surface to whoever owns the assistant.

branches.label: Defer AI Investment
branches.description: Double down on search-and-book UX and performance marketing. Lowest short-term cost; highest risk of being routed around once agents reach mainstream adoption (2029–2031 central estimate).

branches.label: EU AI Act enforcement reshapes agent rollout
branches.description: Counterfactual divergence: in 2029 the EU adopts a strict travel-AI regulatory regime (transparency, bias auditing, liability for agent-made bookings), forcing OTAs to rethink AI agent strategy in their largest discretionary-travel market.

nodes.title: Airline Direct-Booking Tipping Point
nodes.description: Airlines capture >50% of flight bookings directly on domestic routes — propelled by NDC, loyalty-program lock-in, and AI agents that now query airline.com directly. OTAs no longer 'own' the flight transaction and must redefine the value proposition or consolidate.

branches.label: Pivot to Complex Itineraries
branches.description: Focus on multi-city, multi-modal (flight + rail + car + lodge), multi-PNR itineraries that airlines and their AI agents cannot serve directly. Higher margin per trip; smaller addressable market.

branches.label: Become AI-First Aggregator
branches.description: Transform into a marketplace API layer that suppliers plug into and consumer-facing AI agents query. Volume play in a post-consumer-UX world; margin compresses but scale is enormous.

branches.label: Consolidate or Exit
branches.description: M&A to gain scale, sell to a hyperscaler, or exit the market as unit economics compress beyond viable return on ad spend.

nodes.title: EU AI Regulation Shock
nodes.description: The EU enacts binding rules on consumer-facing AI travel agents in 2029 — building on the AI Act, it mandates explainability for ranking/recommendation, audit trails for autonomous bookings, clear liability when an agent mis-books, and restrictions on personal-data-driven personalization. EU represents ~30% of global OTA gross bookings, so the regime effectively sets a global compliance floor. OTAs must decide whether to lead on compliance (turning it into a moat), geo-fence AI features out of the EU, or lobby and stall.

branches.label: Compliance-as-moat
branches.description: Invest heavily in explainable AI, human-in-the-loop booking confirmation, audit logging, and EU-localized data pipelines. Slower feature velocity, higher cost-to-serve, but a regulatory moat against smaller players and a credible trust narrative globally.

branches.label: Geo-fence AI agents out of EU
branches.description: Keep advanced AI concierge features in NA and APAC; revert EU surface to traditional search-and-book to avoid compliance burden. Protects margin short-term; cedes the EU AI consumer surface to compliant local rivals (eDreams, Etraveli) and airline.com.

branches.label: Lobby and stall
branches.description: Coordinate industry lobbying to delay or soften enforcement; minimal product changes pending clarification. Cheapest path if regulators blink; significant fines and reputational damage if enforcement lands hard in 2030–2031.

nodes.source_question: What if the EU regulates the AI usage in 2029?

strategic_profiles.name: Cost Leader
strategic_profiles.description: Volume-and-scale play: wins on commoditized hotel and flight inventory via performance marketing, aggressive cancellation flexibility, and operational efficiency. Typical of budget- focused brands (Agoda, eDreams), subscription-led price players, and flight-only aggregators.

strategic_profiles.name: Differentiator
strategic_profiles.description: Content and experience play: invests in AI-driven personalization, premium UX (Trip Planner, AI concierges), unique inventory (Vrbo / homes), and brand. Defensible against airline direct-booking pressure because the value-add isn't just the ticket — it's itinerary, stay, and ancillary.

strategic_profiles.name: Niche Search
strategic_profiles.description: Technical-moat play: solves a hard specialist problem airlines and hyperscalers can't easily copy — multi-city virtual interlining (Kiwi.com), complex multi-PNR itineraries, regional dominance. Volume is small but margin per booking and customer loyalty are high.

nodes.title: EU AI Act Enforcement on Travel Agents
nodes.description: The European Union enforces binding AI regulations under the EU AI Act specifically targeting AI-powered travel booking agents — classifying them as high-risk systems requiring transparency, human-oversight, and data-localisation compliance. branches.label: Comply & Invest in RegTech
branches.description: Build dedicated EU compliance infrastructure — algorithmic audit trails, human-in-the-loop review, GDPR-aligned data pipelines. High upfront cost but creates a trusted-AI brand signal that differentiates in the EU market and sets a template for other jurisdictions.

branches.label: Geo-fence EU AI Features
branches.description: Temporarily disable or degrade AI agent features for EU users while maintaining full capability in non-EU markets (US, APAC). Minimises compliance cost in the short term but cedes AI-first UX ground to compliant rivals inside the EU.

branches.label: Lobby & Delay Compliance
branches.description: Join industry coalitions to contest or delay enforcement timelines while continuing AI rollout. Risk of regulatory fines and reputational damage; buys 12–18 months but leaves legal uncertainty hanging over EU operations.

nodes.title: EU AI Act (Hypothetical 2015 Enactment) Bites
nodes.description: In this counterfactual, the EU enacted sweeping AI regulation as early as 2015 — a decade ahead of reality. By 2030, strict conformity assessments, mandatory explainability requirements, and liability rules for 'high-risk' AI systems (including automated travel booking agents) have significantly slowed GenAI adoption across European OTAs and airline distribution. US and APAC players operating in the EU face costly compliance overhead, while EU-headquartered OTAs (Booking Holdings NL, eDreams) bear the heaviest burden. The result is a bifurcated global market: AI-first booking in APAC and North America vs. a heavily regulated, slower-moving European travel tech ecosystem.

branches.label: Comply & Localise

branches.label: Geo-Arbitrage (Route Around EU Rules)
branches.description: Large OTAs shift AI development and data processing to non-EU jurisdictions (US, Singapore), serving EU consumers via compliant front-ends backed by offshore AI engines. Legally grey; exposes firms to enforcement risk but preserves AI capability.

branches.label: EU Market Retreat

branches.label: Embrace NDC + ONE Order

branches.label: Stay GDS-first

nodes.title: AI Agent Gateway Decision

branches.label: Build Proprietary AI Concierge
branches.description: Heavy R&D into in-house conversational booking (successor generations of Expedia Trip Planner, Trip.com TripGenie, Priceline Penny). Owns the consumer surface; defensible moat if adoption lands.

branches.label: Partner with AI Gatekeepers
branches.description: Integrate as the travel-fulfillment backend for OpenAI / Anthropic / Google agents. Fast to market, low R&D burn; gives up the consumer-facing surface to whoever owns the assistant.

branches.label: Defer AI Investment
branches.description: Double down on search-and-book UX and performance marketing. Lowest short-term cost; highest risk of being routed around once agents reach mainstream adoption (2029–2031 central estimate).

branches.label: EU AI Regulation (2015) Constrains Adoption
branches.description: Counterfactual: the EU's hypothetical 2015 AI Act has matured by 2028, imposing strict rules on automated booking agents and high-risk AI systems. branches.label: US Government Invests in OTA AI (2028)
branches.description: A divergent path where US federal and institutional investment flows into OTA AI infrastructure, accelerating GenAI adoption among US-headquartered OTAs and reshaping the competitive dynamics of the global travel tech market.

nodes.title: US Federal & Institutional Investment in OTA AI Solutions
nodes.description: The US government, alongside major institutional investors (DARPA, SBA, venture-backed public funds), launches a strategic investment programme targeting AI-powered OTA infrastructure in 2028. Subsidised R&D grants, tax incentives for AI integration, and public-private partnerships accelerate the deployment of GenAI booking agents, NDC pipelines, and autonomous travel planning tools across US-headquartered OTAs (Expedia, Booking Holdings US ops, Priceline). This creates an uneven global playing field: US OTAs gain a significant cost and capability advantage over EU and APAC rivals, while domestic airline-direct booking pressure is partially offset by AI-enriched OTA value propositions. The key question becomes how US OTAs deploy this windfall — doubling down on consumer AI surfaces, infrastructure APIs, or global expansion.
nodes.source_question: What if the USA starts investment in OTA for their AI solutions in 2028?

branches.label: Double Down on Consumer AI Surfaces
branches.description: US OTAs funnel federal grants and tax incentives into next-generation conversational AI travel agents — hyper-personalised, voice-first, and deeply integrated with loyalty programmes. Consumer switching costs rise sharply; US OTAs reclaim market share lost to airline-direct booking and international rivals.

branches.label: Build Open AI Infrastructure & API Layer
branches.description: Investment is channelled into open, standards-based AI booking infrastructure (NDC-native APIs, AI agent protocols, interoperability frameworks). US OTAs become the backbone of global travel AI, monetising as B2B infrastructure providers rather than consumer-facing brands. High scale, moderate margin.

branches.label: Global Expansion & Geopolitical Rivalry
branches.description: US OTAs use investment backing to aggressively expand into APAC and LATAM markets, directly challenging Trip.com and MakeMyTrip. Geopolitical tensions rise as China responds with protectionist measures. Short-term market share gains in neutral markets; elevated regulatory and operational risk.

branches.label: Investment Misallocated / Regulatory Capture
branches.description: Political dysfunction and lobbying from incumbent GDS players dilute the programme. Funds are spread thin across too many recipients, compliance overhead eats into R&D budgets, and the expected AI leap fails to materialise on schedule. US OTAs see modest gains but no transformative capability shift.

nodes.title: Indian Market OTA Monopoly — Post-80% Share Dominance
nodes.description: By 2032, a single OTA (likely MakeMyTrip or a consolidated entity) has captured over 80% of India's rapidly expanding online travel market — now the world's third-largest by volume, driven by the rise of India's middle class, UPI-enabled instant payments, and aggressive AI-personalisation. This dominant position triggers a cascade of downstream decisions: Indian antitrust regulators (CCI) launch investigations, global rivals eye India as a growth market, and the dominant OTA must decide whether to leverage its monopoly to expand regionally or risk forced break-up. The rest of the global OTA industry faces a strategic fork: attempt re-entry into India, build APAC coalitions, or cede the subcontinent and focus on other high-growth corridors.

branches.label: Dominant OTA Expands Regionally (APAC Push)
branches.description: Riding its 80% Indian market share, the dominant OTA deploys capital into Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and Africa — replicating the India playbook of AI-first, mobile-native, UPI-equivalent payment rail integration. Global OTA incumbents face a well-capitalised new challenger in markets they had taken for granted. Indian domestic airline direct-booking pressure remains contained by the OTA's loyalty lock-in.

branches.label: CCI Breaks Up the Monopoly — Forced Restructuring
branches.description: India's Competition Commission (CCI), responding to supplier complaints and consumer fare inflation, orders structural separation — splitting the dominant OTA's flights, hotels, and package arms into independent entities. Global rivals (Booking Holdings, Expedia) re-enter India through targeted acquisitions of the separated units. Market share re-fragments; innovation temporarily stalls during legal and operational restructuring.

branches.label: Global OTAs Form India Coalition / Joint Venture
branches.description: Faced with a near-impenetrable 80% share incumbent, Booking Holdings, Expedia, and Trip.com form a rare joint venture — pooling inventory, AI infrastructure, and marketing spend — to compete collectively in India. The coalition captures the remaining 20% and slowly erodes the leader's share through superior GenAI trip planning and NDC-powered flight retailing. Antitrust risk shifts to the coalition itself in Western markets.

branches.label: Airline Direct Booking Erodes OTA Dominance in India
branches.description: IndiGo, Air India (Tata-owned and AI-enabled), and Akasa Air accelerate direct booking programmes with dynamic NDC pricing, exclusive loyalty rewards, and AI agents. India's dominant OTA finds its 80% share hollowed out from the supply side — airlines reclaim the flight vertical, leaving the OTA reliant on hotels and packages. Market share erodes back to 55–60%, resetting competitive dynamics.

strategic_profiles.description: Technical-moat play: solves a hard specialist problem airlines and hyperscalers can't easily copy — multi-city virtual interlining (Kiwi.com), complex multi-PNR itineraries, regional dominance. Volume is small but margin per booking and customer loyalty are high.
